coredata.dc:description: In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence hydrogel nanosensor was developed by integrating a composite consisting of o-phenylenediamine (OPD), manganese dioxide nanoflakes (MnO2 NFs), and N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) into an agarose hydrogel for sulfite detection. MnO2 NFs demonstrated intense oxidase-like activity, facilitating the conversion of non-fluorescent OPD into yellow-emissive 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). As a result, a significant emission peak belongs to DAP, alongside the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs through FRET. Upon interaction with sulfite, MnO2 NFs lost their oxidase-like function. This process decreased the fluorescence of DAP and restored the blue fluorescence of N-CDs, producing a typical ratiometric response, ranging from 3 nM ∼ 400 μM, with a detection limit (LOD) of 3.79 nM. Employing a smartphone, the fluorescence color change demonstrated by the hydrogel sensor was translated into quantitative data (LOD: 8.44 nM). This hydrogel sensor offers an affordable, portable, and user-friendly solution for sulfite detection and food safety monitoring.

head.ce:title: Nanozyme-mediated ratiometric fluorescence hydrogel for on-site detection of sulfite in food

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, a ratiometric fluorescence hydrogel nanosensor was developed by integrating a composite consisting of o-phenylenediamine (OPD), manganese dioxide nanoflakes (MnO2 NFs), and N-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) into an agarose hydrogel for sulfite detection. MnO2 NFs demonstrated intense oxidase-like activity, facilitating the conversion of non-fluorescent OPD into yellow-emissive 2,3-diaminophenazine (DAP). As a result, a significant emission peak belongs to DAP, alongside the fluorescence quenching of N-CDs through FRET. Upon interaction with sulfite, MnO2 NFs lost their oxidase-like function. This process decreased the fluorescence of DAP and restored the blue fluorescence of N-CDs, producing a typical ratiometric response, ranging from 3 nM ∼ 400 μM, with a detection limit (LOD) of 3.79 nM. Employing a smartphone, the fluorescence color change demonstrated by the hydrogel sensor was translated into quantitative data (LOD: 8.44 nM). This hydrogel sensor offers an affordable, portable, and user-friendly solution for sulfite detection and food safety monitoring.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Examining food additive content is an important and routine test item for food safety and quality supervision. Sulfite is a familiar food additive for antimicrobial (Lamas, Miranda, Vázquez, Cepeda, & Franco, 2016), preventing browning (Kuijpers et al., 2012), and extending shelf life (Treesuwan et al., 2022). However, excessive intake of sulfite disrupts human metabolic balance, resulting in various adverse effects such as headaches, respiratory distress, allergic reactions, and potentially lung cancer (Maiti, 2022). Given the documented detrimental impacts on the well-being of humans, the levels of sulfite in food and medicine are strictly regulated in several countries. For instance, in China, the maximum allowed concentration of sulfite in white granulated sugar is strictly controlled to be <0.1 mg/kg (calculated as SO2). Based on these considerations, it is imperative to establish a straightforward and efficient approach for the precise determination of sulfite in order to uphold the integrity and safety of food.

Conventional methods such as colorimetry (Yue, Fu, Wu, Xu, & Bai, 2023), chromatography (Yoshikawa, Uekusa, & Sakuragawa, 2015), and titration (Rajantie & Williams, 2001) have historically been employed for monitoring sulfite levels in food. Despite their high accuracy, their reliance on toxic solvents, complex sample pretreatment, bulky equipment, and time-consuming processes make them unsuitable for on-site testing. In comparison to traditional detection methods, on-site detection has advantages in cost-effectiveness, high efficiency, and robust capabilities for real application. In resource-limited areas, the availability of simple, cost-effective, and portable equipment for on-site detection is essential for enhancing living standards. To facilitate the implementation of on-site detection, it is necessary to prioritize the development of innovative detection methods and corresponding portable equipment. The rapid advancements in nanotechnology and materials science have significantly contributed to the emergence of a variety of portable, miniaturized, and low-cost inspection tools (Arabi et al., 2021; Ostovan et al., 2022), which are well-suited for diverse on-site applications. On-site testing devices should not only be portable but also optimize analytical procedures, enabling both quantitative and qualitative determinations of sulfite. Optical miniaturized sensors have the unique advantages of high sensitivity, low cost, and simple operation mode (Arabi et al., 2021, 2022). Notably, ratiometric fluorescence nano-probes offer advantages of dynamic range and systematic error elimination through integrated self-calibration. Moreover, this kind of probes generate distinct colors with characteristic hues for analyte detection, can reduce visual fatigue, facilitating visual semi-quantitative assessments and enableing precise quantitative measurements via smartphone applications.

Recently, nanomaterial-based oxidase-like enzymes have garnered significant research interest, particularly because their catalytic processes do not necessitate the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) (Chi, Zhu, Jing, Wang, & Lu, 2019). Among these, ultrathin two-dimensional (2D) transition metal nanoflakes, characterized by a high surface-to-volume ratio, offered advantages such as reduced diffusion barriers and increased accessibility of active sites for substrates, leading to improved sensitivity in fluorescence sensors. Despite OPD-MnO2 NFs have been applied in the detection field, primary research was limited within laboratory settings, which did not fulfill the requirements for rapid batch on-site detection, and the underlying sensor detection mechanisms remained poorly discovered (Rahimi & Anbia, 2023; Yao et al., 2017). Great efforts also have been devoted to the construction of fluorescence bioprobes for sulfite detection based on nano-enzymes (Li et al., 2023; Zhang, Xue, & Feng, 2016), yet few studies have focused on on-site detection, and the majority of these methods lack portability, thereby limiting their effectiveness for real-time sulfite sensing. Therefore, it is imperative to develop a portable device for on-site sulfite testing.

Hydrogels with interpenetrating polymeric networks are featured with high mechanical stability, high loading capacity, and flexibility (Luo et al., 2021). Hydrogels facilitate the homogeneous distribution of nanomaterials, effectively mitigating aggregation-induced quenching (Qu et al., 2022) and the “coffee ring” effect on paper-based strips (Liu, Wang, Jiang, & Zhao, 2024). To our best knowledge, there has not been any research on hydrogel kits for sulfite detection. Additionally, hydrogel matrices provide an inert environment, enhancing nanomaterial stability while enabling the diffusion of target molecules through porous networks. However, visual quantification by the naked eye is constrained to semiquantitative analysis (Wang et al., 2022), often leading to erroneous outcomes. Precise measurements continue to depend on sophisticated instruments, thereby restricting their application in on-site testing. Thus, the integration of smartphones with hydrogel kits offers an option for detection. By analyzing the RGB values of images captured through a smartphone, precise quantification of the analyte can be achieved.

Inspired by the above facts of MnO2 NFs sensors with OPD system (Zhao et al., 2021), and the respective advantages of on-site ratiometric fluorescence hydrogel kits, we constructed a visually intelligent platform for on-site detection of sulfite based on MnO2 NFs and fluorescent N-CDs, and the quenching process and luminescence mechanism were studied comprehensively. MnO2 NFs functioned as both fluorescence quenchers for N-CDs and catalysts for OPD. OPD and N-CDs acted as fluorescence signal substrates. In the absence of sulfite, MnO2 NFs could directly oxidize OPD into DAP, emitting robust yellow luminescence, and concurrently suppressing the fluorescence output of N-CDs via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET). In the presence of sulfite, a cascade reaction was initiated, reducing MnO2 NFs to Mn2+ and inhibiting their oxidase-like activity, thereby suppressing DAP production. The decrease of DAP fluorescence and the recovery of N-CDs fluorescence constitute a typical ratiometric fluorescence response, generating a concentration-dependent visual color change from blue to yellow. Moreover, we designed a target-responsive hydrogel test kit incorporating a dual-emission ratiometric fluorescent probe for precise on-site monitoring. N-CDs, the indicators were embedded into agarose on the cap of an Eppendorf tube to form a hybrid hydrogel. Subsequently, the tube was inverted to allow the diffusion of a mixture containing DAP, MnO2 NFs, and sulfite solutions into the hydrogels. The fluorescent color changes of the portable kit were digitally captured using ImageJ software (Scheme 1C). LOD is significantly lower than the maximum sulfite limit stipulated by the EU (50 mg/kg). Furthermore, the smartphone-integrated ratiometric fluorescence hydrogel sensing platform was successfully employed to test sulfite concentration in food samples, yielding satisfactory results. To our knowledge, the strategy of fabricating a cascade reaction-based ratiometric fluorescence probe and the application of a portable hydrogel kit for sulfite detection has not been previously reported, providing noteworthy benefits with regard to accuracy, sensitivity, and portability. In summary, the ratiometric fluorescent probe demonstrates promising potential as a powerful and efficient tool for on-site monitoring of sulfite in food.

Sodium citrate was purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Tripolycyanamide was supplied by Heowns (Tianjin, China). Sodium sulfite and o-phenylenediamine were sourced from Macklin Biochemical Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Agarose, BSA, and OVA were obtained from SIGMA-ALORICH (Yoshikawa, Uekusa, & Sakuragawa). Tris were purchased from BIOFROXX (Germany). Calcium chloride was obtained from Baishi (Tianjin, China). Magnesium chloride, potassium dihydrogen phosphate, and sodium chloride were sourced from DAMAO (Tianjin, China). Magnesium sulfate was produced by Comet (Guangzhou, China). Zinc nitrate was brought from Chron (Chengdu, China). All chemicals used in this study were of analytical reagent quality. Ultrapure water was purified with Milli-Q water (resistivity >18 MΩcm) from a Millipore system. Grape, white granulated sugar, and biscuits were bought from local supermarkets.
Details are presented in the Supplementary Material.

MnO2 NFs were prepared in accordance with a reported method (Liu, Xu, Sun, & Yin, 2015). First, 100 mL of ultrapure water was used to dissolve 90 μL of 1.8 M H2SO4 solution and 922.8 mg of SDS. The mixture was then agitated at 95 °C for 15 min. The mixture above was then quickly mixed with 3.2 mL of 0.05 M KMnO4 solution, and the temperature was lowered to 85 °C. For 60 min, the mixture was stirred. The resultant dark-brown MnO2 NFs sediments were collected, and the supernatant was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm until it turned clear. Following that, alcohol and ultrapure water were used to wash the supernatant. Ultimately, a colloidal suspension was created by resuspending the acquired MnO2 NFs in ultrapure water, and this was kept at 4 °C for storage.

With minor modifications, N-CDs were synthesized according to the procedure from a previously reported paper (Han, Shi, & Liu, 2017). To summarize, 25 mL of ultrapure water was used to dissolve 0.58 g of sodium citrate and 0.24 g of melamine. The mixture was then sonicated for ten minutes. The mixture was then placed into a 50 mL hydrothermal reactor and heated for 12 h at 180 °C. After cooling, the reaction mixture was filtered through a 0.22 μm cylindrical membrane filter to exclude large particles. The prepared N-CDs solution was then stored at 4 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Ratiometric fluorescent assay for sensing of sulfite
ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence detections of sulfite were conducted in 96-well plates. Initially, 50 μL of 4 mM OPD solution and 20 μg/mL MnO2 NFs were sequentially added to 50 μL of various concentrations of sulfite standard solution. After that, the combinations were left to incubate for 20 min at room temperature. Following this, 50 μL of carbon dots (diluted 2000 times) were added to the solutions and incubated for three more minutes at room temperature. The 360 nm excitation wavelength was used to acquire the fluorescence spectra. Anti-interference investigations were carried out using the sensing system. The relationship between the concentration of sulfite (ranging from 3 nM to 400 μM) and the fluorescence intensity ratio (F455/F575) was systematically investigated. All experiments were performed in triplicate for accuracy and reliability.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of portable hydrogel test kit
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, 50 mg of agarose was added to 4 mL of ultrapure water heated to 100 °C in a water bath and stirred continuously until completely dissolved. Once the solution reached approximately 45 °C, 1 mL of N-CDs (diluted 2000 times) was injected and vigorously stirred until uniformly distributed. The resulting agarose gel had a concentration of 1 %. The mixture was subsequently distributed equally into tube caps and let solidify for five minutes at room temperature. After that, the assembled kit could be kept at 4 °C for later use.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Visual determination of sulfite by a portable hydrogel-based kit
ce:section.ce:para: For on-site detection of sulfite, 1.5 mL centrifuge tubes maintaining 50 μL of different sulfite concentrations were first filled with 50 μL of MnO2 NFs (20 μg/mL) and OPD (4 mM). After incubating for 20 min at room temperature, the centrifuge tubes were inverted to allow the reaction mixture with different sulfite concentrations to diffuse into the hydrogel and quench N-CDs. Following 5 min of incubation at room temperature, the color changes of the hydrogel under ultraviolet light were recorded by smartphone and analyzed using ImageJ software. Convert optical image information to color modulation parameter of R/B, to investigate the linear correlation between the concentration of sulfite and the ratio of R to B.

All food specimens were purchased from local supermarkets. The amounts of sulfite in white granulated sugar, grapes, and biscuits were measured to confirm the practicability of the probe. Using spiking recovery experiments, the accuracy of the proposed method was assessed. Spiked samples were prepared: 2.5 g of white granulated sugar, 2.5 g of stemmed grapes, and 2.5 g of biscuits were spiked with certain concentrations of sulfite standard solutions. To guarantee total sulfite absorption, the spiked samples were kept at room temperature for 60 min and then stored overnight at 4 °C, followed by the pretreatment. Initially, a solution of 2.5 g white granulated sugar in 25 mL ultrapure water was filtered through a 0.22 μm microporous membrane. 5.0 g of peeled grape pulp was homogenized, and 2.5 g of the resulting pulp was mixed with 20 mL ultrapure water. The mixture was centrifuged for 10 min at 10,000 rpm, and the supernatant was transferred to a different centrifuge tube. After that, a 0.45 μm filter was used to remove any remaining solid particles from the extract. After that, a 2.5 g sample of uniformly ground biscuits was stirred for 15 min in 25 mL of ultrapure water. The procedure was done twice after the supernatant was collected following ten minutes of centrifugation at 10,000 rpm. Furthermore, 10 mL of petroleum ether was added, vortexed for a minute, then centrifuged for 10 min at 9000 rpm. After discarding the top organic phase, the extraction procedure was carried out twice more. After that, a 0.22 μm microporous membrane was used to filter the collected supernatant which was diluted in a working buffer (0.05 mM, pH 8.0, Tris-HCl) 10 times for the grape, 10 times for the white granulated sugar, and 20 times for the biscuit extraction solutions, respectively, for fluorescence analysis. The pretreated samples were then analyzed using the on-site hydrogel kit and the developed ratiometric fluorescence probe system.

In comparison to natural enzymes, inorganic materials utilized as enzyme mimics offer several advantages, including straightforward preparation, cost-effectiveness, and enhanced stability (Chi et al., 2019). MnO2, an inorganic metal oxide, is particularly appealing due to its low cost, low toxicity, and high natural abundance. Additionally, MnO2 NFs possess a high specific surface area, enabling efficient oxidation of substrates to yield fluorescent products that facilitate visual detection through simulated oxidase activity (Zhao et al., 2021). Recent advancements in FRET-based fluorescence sensors have underscored their ability to provide real-time, noninvasive monitoring of fluorescence signal changes, thereby illustrating the spatial and temporal distributions of fluorescent materials (Hiremath, Banerjee, & Chatterjee, 2022). Consequently, the chromogenic substrate OPD was selected for this study, as its oxidation product, DAP, exhibited fluorescence, allowing for the establishment of a ratio fluorescence system in conjunction with N-CDs. Notably, N-CDs stand out against conventional fluorophores due to their exceptional photostability, chemical inertness, and cost-effectiveness (Kong et al., 2017).

ce:section.ce:section-title: The feasibility and working mechanisms of the proposed probe for sulfite analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibility of the probe for detecting sulfite was confirmed through several experiments. OPD served as the traditional fluorogenic substrate due to its facile oxidation to the fluorescent product DAP under the catalyzation of oxidase, while MnO2 NFs have been demonstrated as an effective oxidase-like nanozyme capable of directly catalyzing the oxidation of typical oxidase substrates (Zhao et al., 2021). Initially, the oxidase-like activity of the MnO2 NFs synthesized was evaluated. As depicted in Fig. 1 A the MnO2 NFs displayed oxidase-like activity through the oxidation of OPD to DAP, resulting in an apparent yellow fluorescence emission at 575 nm (depicted by the yellow line). Simultaneously, a transition in the solution color from colorless to yellow was noticed, accompanied by the emergence of a distinct absorption peak characteristic of DAP around 420 nm (Fig. 1B). The results obtained in this study have successfully demonstrated the oxidation of OPD initiated by MnO2 NFs. Subsequently, the inhibitory effect of N-CDs was also confirmed. As demonstrated in Fig. 1C, the emission peak of N-CDs at 455 nm experienced a notable reduction when MnO2 NFs were introduced. Furthermore, in the simultaneous presence of N-CDs and OPD solution, only the emission from N-CDs at 455 nm was observed. However, with the addition of MnO2 NFs, a decrease in the fluorescence intensity at 455 nm was noted, accompanied by the emergence of a new peak at 575 nm, which is attributed to the DAP formation. Moreover, as depicted in Fig. 1D, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs did not vary with the presence of sulfite, indicating a lack of interaction between sulfite and N-CDs. Subsequent investigations focused on the impact of sulfite on the decomposition of MnO2 NFs. In the presence of sulfite, MnO2 NFs were reduced to Mn2+, resulting in the attenuation of fluorescence quenching and catalytic activities. Spectral analysis depicted in Fig. 1B revealed a decrease in the absorption peak of MnO2 NFs in the presence of sulfite, indicating the breakdown of MnO2 NFs. Fluorescence spectroscopy was employed to characterize the system, demonstrating a significant reduction in the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 575 nm following the addition of sulfite. Concurrently, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 455 nm increased when sulfite was introduced into N-CDs/OPD/MnO2 mixture system. The complementary changes in the signals of DAP and N-CDs validated the feasibility of developing this ratiometric fluorescent probe. The experimental findings confirmed the efficacy of this fluorescent probe in detecting sulfite, making it a promising tool for analytical applications.

The fluorescence response mechanism of the composite system comprising N-CDs, OPD, and MnO2 NFs was confirmed through UV–visible absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence spectroscopy, analysis of fluorescence lifetime changes, and measurements of zeta potential. Initially, we thoroughly investigated the catalytic kinetics of the MnO2 NFs-OPD reaction by adjusting the concentrations of OPD while keeping the MnO2 NFs concentration constant. Notably, the MnO2 NFs demonstrated catalytic activity in the oxidation of OPD to generate yellow DAP even in the absence of hydrogen peroxide. The Michaelis-Menten constant (km) was determined by computational analysis based on the Lineweaver-Burk double reciprocal plot:

where vmax represents the peak response velocity, while [s] is the concentration of the substrate, and v signifies the initial velocity. The value of km, reflecting the substrate-binding capacity of the enzyme, was determined to be 1.21 mM (refer to Fig. 1E). The findings suggest that MnO2 NFs exhibit favorable oxidase-like characteristics.

The wavelengths of peak excitation and emission for N-CDs were found to be 355 nm and 455 nm, respectively. N-CDs exhibited strong fluorescence, which was significantly attenuated in the presence of MnO2 NFs and DAP. To investigate the possible quenching mechanisms responsible for N-CDs fluorescence by DAP and MnO2 NFs, a series of experiments were performed. As depicted in Fig. 1F, the fluorescence emission spectrum of N-CDs overlapped with the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of DAP within the 400–500 nm range, suggesting that the quenching of N-CDs could potentially occur through IFE and/or FRET (Ahmad et al., 2018). These mechanisms depend on a large overlap between the absorption band of the quenching substance and the emission and/or excitation wavelengths of the fluorescent substance. The analysis presented in Fig. 1G demonstrated that the zeta potential of N-CDs was −7.83 ± 0.16 mV, which diminished to −4.19 ± 0.12 mV upon the introduction of MnO2 NFs-OPD into N-CDs solution. This suggested a potential electrostatic interaction between N-CDs and DAP, facilitating the generation of FRET. The negatively charged surface of N-CDs, characterized by -COOH and -OH groups that act as proton donors, was ascertained through zeta potential measurements. Consequently, DAP is capable of acting as an energy acceptor, receiving energy from N-CDs and leading to fluorescence quenching. After DAP was introduced, the fluorescence lifetime of N-CDs reduced from 7.14 ns to 6.37 ns, as illustrated in Fig. 1H, providing further support for the proposed quenching mechanisms.

Moreover, MnO2 NFs can also serve as quenchers for N-CDs, augmenting the fluorescence quenching effect in tandem with DAP. First and foremost, there was considerable overlap between the emission spectra of N-CDs and the UV–vis absorption spectrum of MnO2 NFs (Fig. 1F), suggesting that the primary quenching mechanisms involve either IFE or FRET (Zhang, Zhou, Tang, Hou, & Wu, 2019). Moreover, the data illustrated in Fig. 1D indicated that the absorption spectra of N-CDs remained stable in the presence of MnO2 NFs, which suggested that no complex formation occurred between these two components. The electrostatic repulsion resulting from the negatively charged N-CDs, which have a zeta potential of −7.83 mV, and the MnO2 NFs, exhibiting a zeta potential of −14.1 mV, effectively prevented any significant interaction. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. 1H, incorporating MnO2 NFs did not markedly alter the fluorescence lifetime of the N-CDs. This observation indicates that the quenching mechanism was not attributed to processes such as FRET, dynamic quenching, or photo-induced electron transfer, as these mechanisms typically lead to fluctuations in the fluorescence lifetime (Qu et al., 2022). Therefore, the MnO2 NFs-triggered fluorescence quenching of N-CDs might be due to IFE. Additional evidence supporting this hypothesis was the gradual occurrence of an anti-Stokes shift in the fluorescence peak, which was attributed to the more pronounced IFE that could distort the fluorescence spectra (Fig. S4) (Jiang et al., 2023). In order to investigate the hypothesis further, corrections for IFE were calculated by considering the cell geometry and absorption features of the aqueous solution containing MnO2 NFs and N-CDs, as stated in eq. 1 (Liu et al., 2015).

The corrected fluorescence (Fcor) is then calculated by deducting the IFE from the observed fluorescence (Fobsd). As illustrated in Fig. 3I, the cuvette width is indicated by d (d = 1.00 cm), the excitation beam thickness is given as s (s = 0.50 cm), and g (g = 0.20 cm) is the distance between the excitation beam and cuvette edges.

The plots depicting the relationship between the observed and adjusted fluorescence of N-CDs quencher against the concentration of quencher (Fig. 1I) suggested that the IFE played a significant role in fluorescence quenching, accounting for approximately 50 % of the overall quenching efficiency for MnO2 NFs.

In the case of MnO2 NFs, it has been demonstrated that they possess the ability to catalyze the conversion of colorless OPD to yellow-emitting DAP (Fig. 1C). Upon exposure to sulfite, MnO2 NFs underwent etching, leading to the formation of Mn2+ through a reduction process. The underlying mechanism leading to the restoration of fluorescence was proved by examining the UV–vis absorbance spectra of solutions containing solely MnO2 NFs compared to those containing MnO2 NFs in conjunction with sulfite, as illustrated in Fig. 1B. A notable absorption peak near 380 nm was detected in the presence of MnO2 NFs. However, this peak disappeared when sulfite was introduced, suggesting that the sulfite ions facilitated the reduction of MnO2 NFs to Mn2+. This observation can be attributed to the ability of sulfite to mediate the transformation of MnO2 into Mn2+.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of N-CDs and MnO2 NFs
ce:section.ce:para: The as-prepared N-doped CDs presented excellent stability and intense blue fluorescence meeting the requirement of detection, which was examined by long-time monitoring of fluorescent intensity. As Fig. S1 depicted, under an 1 h continuous monitor, N-CDs exhibited stable fluorescence intensity. Secondly, when exposed to light for 24 h, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs and hydrogel remained almost unchanged (Fig. S2). These results demonstrated satisfactory fluorescence properties of N-CDs. TEM was used to examine the size distribution and morphology of N-CDs. The inset of Fig. 2 A illustrates the remarkable blue fluorescence that the N-CDs aqueous dispersion displayed when exposed to UV light. The picture in Fig. 2A showed that the N-CDs had a spherical shape and were evenly distributed. As demonstrated in Fig. 2A, the CD particle sizes were mostly centralized in a narrow range between 2.8 and 3.2 nm, with an average size of around 3.22 nm. High-resolution TEM imaging of N-CDs revealed a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, corresponding to the (100) diffraction plane of graphite, suggesting a graphite-like structure (Fig. 2B). The zeta potential measurement revealed a negative value of −7.83 mV (Fig. 2C), attributed to the negatively charged hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CDs, promoting high stability and preventing aggregation through electrostatic repulsion. Fluorescence spectroscopy was used to further clarify the optical characteristics of N-CDs. As illustrated in Fig. 2D, the optimal excitation and emission signals were determined to be 360 nm and 455 nm, respectively. Subsequently, XPS was utilized to examine the elemental composition and surface states of N-CDs. Oxygen, nitrogen, and carbon were detected in N-CDs according to the full-range XPS spectra, which showed distinct peaks at 532.0 eV, 399.0 eV, and 284.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 2E) (Ding, Yu, Wei, & Xiong, 2015). The XPS analysis of O 1 s (Fig. 2F) exhibited peaks at 530.88 eV and 535.61 eV, attributed to -C=O and Na KLL Auger, respectively. Additionally, the N 1 s band (Fig. 2G) revealed peaks corresponding to pyridine N (398.89 eV), C-NH2 (399.91 eV), and C-N-C (398.89 eV). Peaks at 288.32, 287.69, and 284.8 eV were found by deconvolution of the high-resolution C 1 s spectra (Fig. 2H), which correspond to CO, CN, and C-C/C=C, in that order. The surface composition of N-CDs was further investigated by FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2I). The NH and OH stretching vibrations were attributed to the observed peaks at 3418 cm−1 and 3117 cm−1, respectively, and they helped to stabilize N-CDs in aqueous solution. Stretching vibrations of CC and CO were linked to the peak at 1554 cm−1, whereas stretching vibrations of CO and CN were indicated by the peaks at 1014 cm−1 and 1398 cm−1, respectively. In line with the XPS results, the FT-IR data verified the existence of hydroxyl and amine groups on N-CDs surface.

During synthesizing MnO2, a high reaction rate results in the formation of large precipitates composed of nonoriented particles from homogenous nucleation. Reaction rate inhibition is crucial for producing well-shaped and well-dispersed MnO2 NFs by preventing homogenous MnO2 nucleation. Consequently, the approach used herein (Liu, Xu, et al., 2015), which involves the progressive hydrolysis of SDS, guarantees the regulated production of single-layer nanosheets and the slow progression of the redox process. According to Fig. 1E, the oxidative activity of MnO2 NFs was satisfactory for detection. The synthesis of MnO2 NFs was characterized using various analytical techniques including TEM, UV–visible spectroscopy, and XPS. TEM imaging (Fig. 3 A) displayed the distinctive 2D morphology of the MnO2 NFs, characterized by a translucent, ultrathin lamellar structure with folds similar to graphene. The broad absorption band observed in the Fig. 3UV–visible spectrum at 380.01 nm (Fig. 3B) was attributed to d-d transitions within the Mn(IV) ions of the MnO6 octahedra (Liu, Xu, et al., 2015). In the XPS spectrum, the presence of Mn 2p, C 1 s, O 1 s, and N 1 s can be observed clearly (Wang et al., 2016) (Fig. 3C). XPS analysis of Mn (Fig. 3D) indicated characteristic peaks at 653.6 and 641.9 eV, corresponding to Mn 2p1/2 and Mn 2p3/2 of MnO2, with a 11.7 eV spin energy separation, consistent with previous reports on MnO2 (Li et al., 2019). These comprehensive analyses collectively confirmed the successful synthesis of MnO2 NFs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization of the ratiometric detection conditions for sulfite
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence response of the sensing system depends on a variety of experimental variables. To optimize fluorescence output, several critical parameters were optimized, including the concentration of MnO2 NFs, the concentration of OPD, the reaction duration between MnO2 NFs and OPD, as well as the concentrations and pH of the Tris-HCl buffer used. As illustrated in Fig. 4 A, an increase in MnO2 NFs concentration correlated with more intensive oxidation of OPD to DAP. The ratio of fluorescence intensity denoted as F/F0 (with F = (F455/F575) and F0 = (F455/F575)0, indicating the fluorescence intensity ratios in the presence and absence of 10 μM sulfite, respectively), reached its maximum at a concentration of 20 μg/mL of MnO2 NFs. This observation implies that the oxidation of OPD was effectively completed. However, further increase in MnO2 NFs concentration caused significant quenching of N-CDs fluorescence, leading to a reduction in the ratio of fluorescence intensity. As a result, 20 μg/mL was chosen as the ideal concentration of MnO2 NFs for further examination. The concentration of OPD was subsequently optimized. As shown in Fig. 4B, the fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) exhibited an increasing trend with higher OPD concentrations, stabilizing at 4 mM, thereby indicating enhanced oxidation of OPD to DAP and intensified blue fluorescence. Thus, 4 mM was determined to be the optimal concentration for OPD. The reaction duration between MnO2 NFs and OPD was also assessed for optimization. As depicted in Fig. 4C, the fluorescence at 575 nm progressively increased with prolonged reaction time, achieving a steady state approximately at 20 min, signifying the completion of the reaction between MnO2 NFs and OPD. Therefore, a reaction time of 20 min was established to ensure reliable analytical results. Additionally, the pH of the sensing system emerged as an essential parameter influencing the performance of the ratiometric fluorescence probe. As demonstrated in Fig. 4D and Fig. 4E, the solution exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity ratio (F/F0) at a buffer pH of 8 and a concentration of 60 mM. pH 8 was chosen as the optimal pH value for the sensing system in accordance with the fact that the autoxidation of OPD naturally occurred under alkaline conditions (Wang et al., 2019). In addition, the decrease in ratiometric fluorescence response, when pH > 8, might be due to the fact that the MnO2 NFs exhibit oxidase-like activities in an acidic medium, while the corresponding activities are greatly weakened under alkaline conditions (Han et al., 2017). Based on these results, optimal detection performance was achieved with a MnO2 NFs concentration of 20 μg/mL, an OPD concentration of 4 mM, a reaction time of 20 min between MnO2 NFs and OPD, and a Tris-HCl buffer at pH 8.0 with a concentration of 60 mM.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analytical performance of N-CDs/OPD/MnO2 NFs-based probe for sulfite
ce:section.ce:para: Under optimal working conditions, ratiometric fluorescence (F455/F575) was employed for sulfite determination. In Fig. 4G, the fluorescence intensity of N-CDs at 455 nm exhibited an increase, while the fluorescence intensity of DAP at 575 nm showed a decrease in response to escalating sulfite concentrations. Fig. S4 showcased a distinct shift in fluorescence color under UV light, transitioning from a vibrant blue to a yellow fluorescence as sulfite levels increased. The fluorescence intensity ratio (F455/F575) demonstrated a linear correlation with the logarithmically transformed sulfite concentrations spanning from 3 nM to 400 μM (Fig. 4F). The regression equation, y = 0.0225× + 2.456, yielded a high coefficient of determination (R2 = 0.9957). LOD was calculated as 3.79 nM using the 3 d/s rule, where s denotes the slope of the linear calibration curve and d stands for the standard deviation of the blank. The blank standard deviation was calculated by three parallel experiments without adding sulfite. The intrinsic reference signals inherent in N-CDs and DAP ensured self-correction capabilities, effectively mitigating the influence of environmental and background variations, thereby reducing erroneous outcomes. Furthermore, the utilization of the International Commission on Illumination (CIE) 1931 chromaticity diagram (Fig. 4H) substantiated the noticeable shift towards yellow fluorescence as sulfite concentrations increased, confirming the exceptional sensitivity of the dual-emission N-CDs/OPD/MnO2 NFs-based ratiometric fluorescence sensing system in detecting minute quantities of sulfite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: On-site detection of sulfite by hydrogel test kit
ce:section.ce:para: In order to facilitate sulfite on-site detection, an intelligent portable hydrogel test kit was developed by immobilizing N-CDs/OPD mixture into the hydrogel to enhance monitoring capabilities. Agarose is a kind of natural polysaccharide, mainly extracted from seaweeds. It is composed of long chains in which β-D-galactose and 3,6-anhydro-L-galactose are alternately connected. These long chains dissolve and form random coil conformations when heated in water above 90 °C. When the temperature drops to 35–40 °C, some of the chains transform into helical conformations. Subsequently, the resulting helical parts tend to aggregate with the helical parts of other chains through hydrogen bonds, serving as the cross-linking of the agarose chain gel network (Ogawa, 2023). Agarose hydrogel, as a robust support, could be formed by intermolecular forces without complex synthesis or toxic cross-linking agents, which is conservationally friendly (Luo, Chen, Megharaj, & Naidu, 2016). Scheme 1 illustrates the sulfite detection process utilizing the portable test kit. Within the microtube cavity, sulfite inhibited the activity of MnO2 NFs, preventing OPD from oxidizing into DAP, thereby reducing the quenching effect on N-CDs in the agarose hydrogel. Consequently, as the concentration of sulfite increased, in the agarose hydrogel placed on the snap cap, there were distinct color-gradient changes from blue to yellow (Fig. 5 A).

To interpret the fluorescence color response of the hydrogel, we recorded fluorescence images of the test kit snap cap with a smartphone and evaluated them by calculating the ratio of the red (R) channel to the blue (B) channel using ImageJ software, facilitating on-site quantitative detection of sulfite. In Fig. 5B, with the increase in sulfite concentration, two well-linear regression equations were observed as follows: Y = −0.5829× +0.9633 (R2 = 0.997) and Y = −6.6625× + 1.5441 (R2 = 0.995) for determining the sulfite concentration in the range of 5 nM to 100 μM and, correspondingly, 100 to 800 μM. LOD was computed into 8.44 nM by the 3 d/s rule. As demonstrated in Fig. 5C, the histogram of intensity served as an alternative method for color identification, showing a gradient change in sulfite concentration, with the intensity value increasing from 0.51 to 1.57. The results were also compared to previously reported methods for detecting sulfite in food to gauge their sensitivity.

Fig. 5F and Table S2 showed that the constructed N-CDs/OPD/MnO2 NFs probe was capable of detecting sulfite in a dual mode manner with respectable sensitivity, which was promising when compared to previously reported methods. Furthermore, as shown in Table S2, most studies have predominantly occurred for laboratory scale detection, only Li’s team (Li et al., 2022) developed a single fluorescence hydrogel kit, but failed to incorporate a built-in self-calibration function. Moreover, silicon nanodots (SiNDs), employed in previous research for sulfite detection, exhibit inadequate water solubility, susceptibility to aggregation, and limitations stemming from their indirect band gap, resulting in low quantum yield (Zhang, Wu, Hao, & Shen, 2018). Furthermore, the synthesis conditions for SiNDs are difficult to achieve in underdeveloped areas, necessitating anaerobic conditions (Feng, Liu, Su, Ji, & He, 2014). In this work, the sensor can easily visualize the analysis by providing specific fluorescent images, eliminating the need for professional technicians and expensive equipment, and allowing untrained people to easily perform the analysis without understanding the complex sensing principles involved.

In assessing its practical utility, the selectivity of N-CDs/OPD/MnO2 NFs-based ratiometric system towards sulfite was evaluated. Real sample analysis often involves complex external environments and diverse interferences. To simulate real-world conditions more precisely, a range of potential interfering substances, including common inorganic ions (K+, Na+, Mg2+, Ca2+, Zn2+, SO4 2−, Cl−, and NO3 −), representative proteins (OVA, BSA), and NaCl, were introduced into the system at 100 μM, mixed with the sulfite solution (with a concentration ratio of sulfite to interfering substance set at 1:5). Salt tolerance is one of the most common challenges in nanosensor detection of complex samples. The metal ions are frequently encountered in agricultural products such as fruits, vegetables, and wine, underscoring the need to evaluate the anti-interference capability. As depicted in Fig. 4I, most cations, anions, NaCl, and proteins exhibited fluorescence intensity ratios similar to the buffer, which was attributed to the satisfactory redox properties of MnO2 NFs and optimized working conditions for sulfite detection.
As illustrated by the following equation representing reaction between sulfite and MnO2:

This suggests that the influence of these interferents on sulfite detection using N-CDs/OPD/MnO2 NFs detection platform can be considered negligible, indicating high tolerance limits. Overall, the results indicate that the coexisting substances tested have minimal interference with sulfite detection in N-CDs/OPD/based ratiometric fluorescence system, revealing the satisfactory anti-interference performance in detecting sulfite. The favorable detection performance of this platform can be attributed to its unique characteristics: (i) The ratiometric fluorescence sensing mode incorporates an inherent correction mechanism, minimizing background interference. This effectively enhances both sensitivity and accuracy; (ii) As encapsulating materials, hydrogels function as a barrier to keep interferences out of sample matrices while allowing molecules to permeate through the porous structure. Therefore, these findings confirm that N-CDs/OPD/MnO2 NFs hydrogel, together with the smartphone, constitutes a portable test kit for precise, sensitive, and rapid detection of sulfite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of sulfite in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: For the purpose of assessing the applicability of the proposed ratiometric fluorescence probe for examining real food (biscuits, grapes, and white granulated sugar), sulfite spike recovery tests were carried out (Fig. 5D). Given the potential interference arising from the presence of lipids, proteins, and other constituents in complex dietary matrices, potential interactions could lead to substantial influences on fluorescence readouts. After the previously described sample pretreatment procedures, a dilution strategy was used to mitigate matrix effects since MnO2 NFs in different detection environments (acidic, alkaline, and biological systems) are relatively unstable and prone to aggregation, which affects the sensing performance of the sensor (Zhu, Liu, & Wei, 2021). This strategy provided the detection in an optimized buffer solution for better precision. Following pretreatment, the materials were prepared for the proposed ratiometric fluorescence method by diluting them in Tris-HCl solution using various dilution factors.

As demonstrated by Fig. S3, the ratio of F/F0 of the ratiometric fluorescence response (with 10 μM sulfite added as targets) increasingly approximated that caused by Tris-HCl buffer as dilution factors increased. A 20-fold dilution factor for the biscuit matrix, 10-fold for the grape matrix, and 10-fold for the white granulated sugar matrix were comparable to that induced by Tris-HCl buffer, indicating minimal matrix influence on the ratiometric fluorescence detection. Thus, the pretreated biscuit, grape, and white granulated sugar samples spiked with three different levels of sulfite were diluted by the corresponding folds, and finally analyzed by ratiometric fluorescence and portable hydrogel kits to calculate the recoveries.

The results presented in Fig. 5E demonstrate the fluorescence intensity ratio F455/F575 exhibited a positive correlation with the rising concentrations of sulfite (10, 20, and 40 mg/L). As indicated in Table 1 , in the ratiometric fluorescence assay, the recovery range was 94.17 % to 108.63 %, with RSD < 5.39 % (n = 3). As determined by the hydrogel kits, the recovery range was 87.67 % to 110.38 %, with RSD < 6.67 % (n = 3). Furthermore, the results obtained by the traditional titration method, which is the standard method used in GB5009.34–2022, consistently aligned with these findings. The results revealed a negligible variance in the outcomes derived from the ratiometric fluorescence assessment compared to the utilization of portable hydrogel kits, underscoring the satisfactory applicability of the developed method in the quantification of real samples. Due to the advantages of easy use and excellent stability of the hydrogel, the good operability and high efficiency of the portable kit provide a potential prospect for quantitative field screening of sulfites to ensure food safety. Furthermore, we conducted a comparative analysis of these two methods with other probes and methodologies used for sulfite determination in food samples, as shown in Table S1. Both methods exhibited lower detection limits and wider detection ranges. Moreover, the integration of ratiometric fluorescence portable hydrogel kits into a practical smartphone sensing platform enables on-site determination of sulfite in various foods. Therefore, this sensing platform was competitive with other fluorescent probes in terms of versatility and convenience.

In conclusion, a ratiometric fluorescence sensing probe consisting of OPD, MnO2 NFs, and N-CDs for detecting sulfite in food samples has been successfully developed. By employing N-CDs and DAP as signal indicators, a straightforward and highly promising probe with exceptional anti-interference capability and sensitivity has been established. LOD of the fluorescent probe is 3.79 nM, with a linear range spanning from 3 nM to 400 μM. Additionally, a portable test hydrogel kit was ingeniously constructed using agarose hydrogel as a matrix, facilitating on-site detection of sulfite in conjunction with a smartphone-based fluorescence analysis. With increasing sulfite concentration (from 5 nM to 800 μM), a conspicuous color transition from light blue to bright yellow was observed under a UV lamp, and the LOD in solution was as low as 8.44 nM. Leveraging the ratiometric fluorescence signal, which mitigates interferences from environmental factors, the hydrogel test kit demonstrated high accuracy and reliability in sulfite detection. Moreover, the integration of a smartphone application for analysis enhances the precision of visualization and enables rapid quantitative detection. Furthermore, the probe was successfully applied to food samples, yielding favorable recovery rates ranging from 87.67 % to 110.38 %. To our knowledge, this represents the first instance of a hydrogel-based ratiometric fluorescent platform for sulfite determination, offering a detection limit comparable to previous methods. Distinguished by its portability, cost-effectiveness, and vibrant color displays, this platform holds considerable promise for quantitative on-site testing of sulfite in food.
